keyIdeas: The Aufbau principle: orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy — 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p.
Hund's rule: within a subshell, electrons occupy orbitals singly (with parallel spins) before pairing; this minimises electron–electron repulsion.
The Pauli exclusion principle: each orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite (paired) spins.
For ions, first write the neutral atom configuration then remove electrons from the highest-energy occupied subshell; for transition metals, remove 4s electrons before 3d.
Two special cases up to Z = 36: Cr is [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ (half-filled d is extra stable) and Cu is [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ (full d is extra stable).
Electron configurations can be abbreviated using a noble gas core: e.g. Na = [Ne] 3s¹.

mustKnow.definition: Electrons fill atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy, starting with the lowest available energy orbital.
Within a degenerate subshell, electrons occupy separate orbitals singly before any orbital receives a second electron; all singly occupied orbitals have the same spin direction.
No two electrons in an atom can have an identical set of quantum numbers; an orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins.
A notation specifying how electrons are arranged in shells and subshells, e.g. 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d⁶ for Fe.

markSchemePhrases: Aufbau principle / fill in order of increasing energy
Hund's rule / electrons occupy orbitals singly before pairing
4s fills before 3d / 4s is removed before 3d (in ions)
remove 4s electrons first when forming transition metal ions
commandWords: 'Write' the electron configuration — give the full notation or noble-gas shorthand. 'Explain' why Cr is [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ — reference the extra stability of a half-filled d subshell. 'State' the electron configuration of a named ion — remember to remove 4s before 3d for transition metals.
examinerTips: For transition metal ions, always remove 4s electrons first: Fe²⁺ is [Ar] 3d⁶ (not [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s²).
Cr and Cu are the two exceptions in Period 4 you must memorise: Cr = [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹; Cu = [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹.
When asked for an ion configuration, write the neutral atom first, then explicitly state 'remove n electrons from 4s/3d as appropriate'.
Hund's rule is tested in questions asking about spin diagrams (boxes-and-arrows) — each box is one orbital; arrows up and down represent opposite spins.
The noble-gas shorthand is acceptable in OCR A answers: '[Ar] 3d⁶ 4s²' is fine for Fe. Write [Ar] = 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶.
workedExample:
Write the electron configuration of the Fe²⁺ ion (Z = 26).

Step 1: Neutral Fe (Z = 26):
1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d⁶
or [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s²

Step 2: Fe²⁺ loses 2 electrons. Remove from 4s first (highest energy in the ion):
Fe²⁺: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 3d⁶
or [Ar] 3d⁶
commonTraps: Removing 3d electrons instead of 4s electrons when forming transition metal ions — Fe²⁺ is [Ar] 3d⁶ not [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s².
Forgetting the Cr and Cu exceptions and writing Cr as [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s² instead of [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹.
Violating Hund's rule in a box diagram — pairing electrons in one box before the adjacent empty box in the same subshell.
Writing 3d before 4s in the filling order: 4s fills before 3d, so 4s appears before 3d in the configuration for neutral atoms.
Omitting superscripts in the notation (1s² not 1s2) — missing superscripts lose marks in written notation questions.
